content.full_text:
WALLIN, KRESS, REISMAN, PRICE & DILKES
LAW OFFICES
2800 TWENTY-EIGHTH STREET, SUITE 310
SANTA MONICA, CALIFORNIA 90405-2934
TELEPHONE (213) 450-0582
FAX 310-450850

November 27, 1990
VIA FAX

California Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804
Attention: John Rothman

Re: Request for Opinion

Dear Mr. Rothman:

I am contract City Attorney for the City of Maywood. The
City is in the process of adopting a new Zoning Ordinance. The
new ordinance is designed primarily to reenact the prior
ordinance in easier to understand language and to make changes to
conform to a newly adopted General Plan.

Among the proposed changes are a provision which would
1) change a provision regarding rebuilding after catastrophe
from 50% of assessed value to 50% of appraised market value; 2)
exempt property which is nonconforming as a result of changes in
development standards from the amortization schedule otherwise
applicable; and 3) permit rebuilding of nonconforming property
damaged in a catastrophe which is nonconforming solely as a
result of changes in development standards, without regard to the
percentage of damage.

During the course of the hearings on the new Ordinance, it
was determined that one of the City Council members and one of
the Planning Commissioners each own more than three residential
rental units in the City (four and ten respectively). Each of
their properties is nonconforming as a result of changes in
development standards in 1966. Under the current zoning
ordinance, neither person would be able to rebuild the same
number of units in the event of catastrophic destruction.

A majority of residential properties in the city fail to
meet the development standards adopted in 1966. Other officials own
nonconforming residential property, but none of the others own
more than three residential rental units in the city.

The above situation seems quite similar to that discussed in
Request for Advice letter to David J. Aleshire, City Attorney of
Signal Hill, file A-88-325. That letter would be dispositive if
not for newly adopted regulation 18702.1. That is, the officials
owing more than three residential units would not be members of

WALLIN, KRESS, REISMAN, PRICE & DILKES
LAW OFFICES

California Fair Political Practices Commission
November 27, 1990
Page 2

the public generally. Since the effect on their properties will
almost certainly exceed the greater of $1,000.00 or .5% of value,
they would be required to refrain from participation in decisions
concerning amendment of the above provisions.

However, regulation section 18702.1(a)(3) now provides that
the effect of a decision is material regarding an interest in
real property if the decision involves zoning or rezoning, except
that:

"(E) For purposes of this subdivision, the
terms 'zoning' and 'rezoning' shall refer to the
act of establishing or changing the zoning or land
use designation on the subject property, but shall
not refer to an amendment of an existing zoning
ordinance or other land use regulation (such as
changes in the uses permitted, or development
standards applicable, within a particular zoning
category) which is applicable to all other
properties designated in that category."

The changes proposed will not change the zone of any of the
affected properties. They also will not significantly change
development standards, that having been accomplished in 1966.
What they will do is modify or exempt the application of the
prior development standard changes with regard to all
nonconforming residential properties in the city, including those
owned by the officials.

Question: Does the development standards exception of
regulation 18702.1 apply to the Planning Commissioner and City
Council member who own in excess of three nonconforming rental
units in the city?

Your prompt informal response would be appreciated. The
matter is on the Planning Commission's agenda on November 29,
1990 for an adjourned public hearing and a public hearing is
scheduled before the City Council on December 11th.

Thank you for your anticipated cooperation.

Very truly yours,

CARY S. REISMAN
City Attorney
City of Maywood

CSR:kims
cc: Planning Commission and City Council

sections.question: Does the development standards exception of regulation 1-8702.1 apply to the planning Commissioner and City council member who own in excess of three nonconforming rental units in the city?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether officials who own nonconforming residential rental properties in Maywood are subject to conflict-of-interest disqualification requirements under FPPC regulation 1-8702.1(a)(3)(E) when voting on zoning ordinance amendments that would benefit their properties.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC from a city attorney, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or advice section present. Document discusses proposed changes to zoning ordinance and conflict-of-interest implications under regulation 1-8702.1.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Does the development standards exception of regulation 1-8702.1 apply to the planning Commissioner and City council member who own in excess of three nonconforming rental units in the city?

embedding.summary: City Attorney for Maywood requests FPPC opinion on whether two officials owning nonconforming rental properties must be disqualified from voting on proposed zoning ordinance amendments that would benefit their properties by exempting nonconforming units from amortization requirements.
